summary: Fowler explores why object-oriented programming and iterative/incremental development have been historically linked, despite no inherent technical dependency between them. He argues that OO's fine-grained modularity through classes and polymorphism makes code changes easier, which is essential for IID's approach of growing design alongside code. The Smalltalk community's influence was disproportionately large, bringing both OO concepts and IID-friendly tooling. Social factors also played a role, as OO leaders championed iteration from the start, creating a community where both were assumed defaults.
key_points: OO's fine-grained modularity through encapsulation and polymorphism makes code easier to change, which is essential for iterative development
Smalltalk contributed far more than just OO—its IDE, immediate compilation, and environment features all supported iteration
The Unix and Lisp communities demonstrate that modularity and iteration can exist independently of OO
Social factors matter: OO leaders championed iteration, which shaped the entire community's assumptions
The link between OO and IID is one of affinity, not necessity
money_quotes: The whole point of IID is that you don't have a comprehensive design at the beginning, you grow the design and the code together.
Even though the number of Smalltalkers are (and were) small, they were disproportionately loud.
The leaders of the OO world pushed IID right from the beginning, which is why it's no surprise that the leaders of the agile movement almost entirely are OO people.
When the leadership embraces both movements so fervently that has an effect on the whole community.
Objects provide a very fine grained modularity, where the program is divided into small substitutable modules with separation of interface and implementation.
key_insight: Objects and iteration share a strong affinity because OO's modularity enables the evolutionary design that IID requires, and because the same community leaders championed both approaches from the start.
